stops.name: Hachiko Memorial Statue
stops.shortDescription: A bronze statue honoring the famously loyal Akita dog who waited at Shibuya Station every day for nine years after his owner's death. It is now Tokyo's most popular meeting spot.

stops.name: Shibuya Crossing
stops.shortDescription: Often described as the busiest intersection in the world, sending up to 3,000 people across the street during a single light change. The neon-lit crossing is a global symbol of Tokyo's kinetic energy.

stops.name: Shibuya Sky
stops.shortDescription: A 360-degree open-air observation deck located 229 meters above Shibuya. It offers sweeping views of the crossing below, the Tokyo skyline, and Mount Fuji on clear days.

stops.name: Shibuya Center-Gai
stops.shortDescription: A narrow, incredibly busy pedestrian street stretching away from the crossing. It is the heart of Shibuya's youth culture, packed with fast food, clothing stores, and blaring music.

stops.name: Spain-zaka (Spanish Slope)
stops.shortDescription: A charming, winding pedestrian slope that resembles a narrow Mediterranean street. It is densely packed with tiny boutiques, cafes, and restaurants.

stops.name: Shibuya Parco
stops.shortDescription: A cutting-edge shopping complex that is a mecca for pop culture. Its 'Cyberspace' floor famously houses the official Nintendo Tokyo store and a Pokémon Center.

stops.name: Miyashita Park
stops.shortDescription: A brilliant urban redevelopment that transformed a public park into a massive rooftop oasis atop a sleek shopping and dining complex, complete with a skatepark.

stops.name: Cat Street
stops.shortDescription: The main artery of Ura-Harajuku, a winding pedestrian street renowned for its vibrant street fashion and indie boutiques. Despite its name, you're more likely to spot stylish shoppers than felines here.

stops.name: Meiji Jingu Shrine
stops.shortDescription: Tokyo's grandest Shinto shrine, dedicated to Emperor Meiji. It is surrounded by a dense, tranquil forest of 100,000 trees that completely drowns out the noise of the surrounding city.

stops.name: Yoyogi Park
stops.shortDescription: One of Tokyo's largest city parks, originally the site of the 1964 Olympic Village. It's famous for its sprawling lawns, forested paths, and eclectic weekend gatherings.

stops.name: Takeshita Street
stops.shortDescription: The bustling, neon-hued epicenter of Japan's kawaii (cute) culture. This narrow 400-meter street is packed with crêpe stands, cosplay boutiques, and quirky youth fashion stores.

stops.name: Tokyu Plaza Omotesando Harajuku
stops.shortDescription: A major shopping center instantly recognizable by its spectacular, kaleidoscopic mirrored entrance escalator, which has become one of Tokyo's most popular photo spots.

stops.name: Omotesando
stops.shortDescription: Often referred to as Tokyo's Champs-Élysées, this broad, tree-lined avenue is home to striking modern architecture and flagship luxury boutiques. It offers a sophisticated contrast to Harajuku.

stops.name: Omotesando Hills
stops.shortDescription: A sleek, multi-level shopping complex brilliantly designed by renowned architect Tadao Ando. It features a continuous spiraling walkway lined with high-end fashion stores.
